title: Why some traditions belong in the home, not the museum
summary: Argue whether living cultural traditions are better preserved through daily domestic practice or through institutional display and archival.
topic: Why some traditions belong in the home, not the museum
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) on whether certain cultural traditions — cooking techniques, oral stories, seasonal rituals — are better preserved through domestic practice than through museum display or institutional archiving. Refer to at least one concrete example (e.g. the Vietnamese practice of making bánh chưng at Tết, or the Māori tradition of whakapapa recitation). Use a what-cleft sentence, a concession, and at least one passive construction. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What the Vietnamese custom of making bánh chưng at Tết preserves is not merely a recipe but…
Granted, museums perform an undeniable service when a tradition's living practitioners have…
When a cooking technique is transferred to a glass cabinet, something essential is…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thực hành trong gia đình / sinh hoạt trong nhà
lưu trữ có hệ thống bởi tổ chức

được truyền lại / được chuyển giao

bị tách khỏi bối cảnh
sự đọc thuộc lòng / truyền miệng
tuyển chọn và trưng bày (tại bảo tàng)
hóa thạch / biến thành vật cổ không còn sống
người thực hành / người theo nghề
được thừa kế / được kế thừa
hệ phả / truyền thống kể dòng tộc của người Māori
nghi lễ theo mùa
mã hóa ý nghĩa / chứa đựng ý nghĩa
bị thay thế / bị dời chỗ
ký ức tập thể

hintVocab.phrase: to be reduced to an exhibit
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị thu gọn thành một vật trưng bày
sự tiếp nối qua các thế hệ
thương mại hóa văn hóa
kết cấu của cuộc sống hàng ngày
kiến thức được thể hiện qua hành động cơ thể

sampleText:
What the Vietnamese custom of making bánh chưng at Tết preserves is not merely a recipe but a whole choreography of family obligation: who soaks the glutinous rice, who folds the banana leaves, who stays awake through the night watching the pot. When that process is moved from a kitchen in Hà Nội to a glass case in a heritage museum, the knowledge is still nominally present — the label reads correctly, the photographs are accurate — but the tradition has been stripped of the only context in which it actually functions.

Museums perform an undeniable service. When a tradition's living practitioners have dwindled to a handful of elderly people, institutional archiving may be the only means by which future generations encounter the practice at all. The documentation of endangered languages, the preservation of textile techniques, the recording of oral histories — these are genuinely valuable interventions that would be irresponsible to dismiss.

Granted, a museum exhibit can prompt a revival. There are documented cases where young people, encountering an archived tradition in a national institution, have sought out remaining practitioners and worked to restore the practice to daily life. The exhibit, in those cases, becomes a seed rather than a tomb.

Even so, the default logic of the museum — to select, to freeze, and to display — works against the nature of most living traditions. A tradition survives not by being curated but by being used, argued over, adapted, and passed imperfectly from one pair of hands to the next. The Māori practice of whakapapa recitation is not a recitation of fixed genealogical data: it is a living negotiation of identity, shaped differently in each community and each generation. To archive it is to produce one version at the expense of the ongoing process.

What is lost when domestic practice is displaced by institutional preservation is precisely the generational friction that keeps a tradition alive. Grandmothers who insist on their own variation of a dish, uncles who recite a family story with different names, neighbours who perform a seasonal ritual in ways that contradict the official account — these are not errors. They are the mechanism by which a tradition absorbs new experience and remains inhabitable by the living. No museum can replicate that function, because the museum's purpose is to stabilise what practice keeps deliberately unstable.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: What-cleft to foreground the real subject of an argument
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What the Vietnamese custom of making bánh chưng at Tết preserves is not merely a recipe but a whole choreography of family obligation' — cấu trúc what-cleft chuyển trọng tâm từ chủ thể (phong tục) sang điều thực sự quan trọng (the choreography of obligation). Cụm 'not merely … but …' tăng cường sự tương phản.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What the Vietnamese custom … preserves is not merely a recipe but a whole choreography of family obligation' uses a what-cleft to redirect attention from the tradition itself to what it actually carries. The 'not merely … but …' construction sharpens the contrast between surface and substance.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to depersonalise a process and make it systemic
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'When that process is moved from a kitchen in Hà Nội to a glass case in a heritage museum' — bị động ('is moved') xóa chủ thể, nhấn mạnh rằng sự di chuyển là một quy trình hệ thống, không phải quyết định của cá nhân nào. Điều này cho phép tác giả phê phán quá trình mà không tấn công cá nhân cụ thể.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'When that process is moved from a kitchen in Hà Nội to a glass case in a heritage museum' omits the agent, presenting the transfer as a systemic process rather than a personal choice. This allows the writer to critique the practice without attacking any individual.

sampleTranslationVi:
Điều mà phong tục làm bánh chưng của người Việt vào dịp Tết gìn giữ không chỉ là một công thức nấu ăn mà là cả một vũ điệu của nghĩa vụ gia đình: ai ngâm gạo nếp, ai gói lá chuối, ai thức qua đêm canh nồi. Khi quá trình đó được chuyển từ một căn bếp ở Hà Nội vào một tủ kính trong bảo tàng di sản, kiến thức vẫn còn đó về mặt danh nghĩa — nhãn đề chính xác, ảnh chụp đúng — nhưng truyền thống đã bị tước đoạt khỏi bối cảnh duy nhất mà nó thực sự hoạt động.

Bảo tàng thực hiện một dịch vụ không thể phủ nhận. Khi những người thực hành truyền thống còn lại chỉ là một nắm người cao tuổi, lưu trữ có hệ thống bởi tổ chức có thể là phương tiện duy nhất để các thế hệ tương lai tiếp cận thực hành. Việc ghi chép các ngôn ngữ có nguy cơ tuyệt chủng, bảo tồn kỹ thuật dệt vải, ghi lại lịch sử truyền miệng — đây là những can thiệp có giá trị thực sự mà sẽ là thiếu trách nhiệm nếu bác bỏ.

Phải công nhận, một cuộc triển lãm bảo tàng có thể thúc đẩy sự phục hồi. Có những trường hợp được ghi lại khi những người trẻ, khi gặp một truyền thống được lưu trữ trong một tổ chức quốc gia, đã tìm kiếm những người thực hành còn lại và nỗ lực phục hồi thực hành trong cuộc sống hàng ngày. Cuộc triển lãm, trong những trường hợp đó, trở thành hạt giống chứ không phải ngôi mộ.

Dẫu vậy, logic mặc định của bảo tàng — để lựa chọn, đóng băng và trưng bày — đi ngược lại bản chất của hầu hết các truyền thống còn sống. Một truyền thống tồn tại không phải bằng cách được tuyển chọn mà bằng cách được sử dụng, tranh luận, thích nghi và được truyền lại một cách không hoàn hảo từ đôi tay này sang đôi tay khác. Thực hành đọc whakapapa của người Māori không phải là sự đọc dữ liệu phả hệ cố định: đó là một cuộc đàm phán sống về bản sắc, được định hình khác nhau trong mỗi cộng đồng và mỗi thế hệ. Lưu trữ nó là tạo ra một phiên bản với cái giá phải trả là quá trình liên tục.

Điều gì bị mất khi thực hành trong gia đình bị thay thế bởi sự bảo tồn có hệ thống chính xác là sự ma sát giữa các thế hệ giữ cho truyền thống còn sống. Những người bà khăng khăng theo cách làm riêng của họ, những người chú kể lại câu chuyện gia đình với những cái tên khác nhau, những người hàng xóm thực hiện một nghi lễ theo mùa theo những cách mâu thuẫn với tài khoản chính thức — đây không phải là lỗi. Chúng là cơ chế mà theo đó một truyền thống hấp thụ kinh nghiệm mới và vẫn có thể được người sống cư ngụ. Không bảo tàng nào có thể sao chép chức năng đó, vì mục đích của bảo tàng là ổn định hóa những gì mà thực hành cố ý giữ cho không ổn định.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to highlight what a tradition truly contains?
options: What the Vietnamese custom of making bánh chưng at Tết preserves is not merely a recipe but a whole choreography of family obligation.
The Vietnamese custom preserves many things, most importantly a recipe and also family obligations.
It is the Vietnamese custom which preserves recipes as well as family obligations at Tết.
Bánh chưng is a Vietnamese recipe that also carries family obligations within it.
mcQuestions.explanation: The what-cleft 'What X preserves is not … but …' positions the key phrase at the end and uses 'not merely … but' for contrast.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … preserves is not … but …' — that structure is the cleft.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say happens when bánh chưng-making is moved to a museum?
options: The recipe is lost and cannot be recovered.
The tradition gains a larger audience and becomes more widely known.
The tradition is stripped of the context in which it actually functions.
Young people become more interested in learning the practice.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states the tradition 'has been stripped of the only context in which it actually functions' once moved to a glass case.
mcQuestions.hint: Re-read the first paragraph's final sentence.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, when can museum archiving be genuinely valuable?
options: When the tradition is already very popular and widely practised.
When living practitioners have dwindled and institutional archiving is the only means of transmission.
When the government has funded a major cultural project.
When the tradition involves physical objects that need conservation.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay grants that when practitioners 'have dwindled to a handful of elderly people, institutional archiving may be the only means' of survival.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay's concession paragraph explains exactly this scenario.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'The exhibit, in those cases, becomes a seed rather than a tomb' mean?
options: Museum exhibits are too old to be useful.
An exhibit can inspire revival rather than merely marking a tradition's death.
Seeds are better preserved in museums than in gardens.
The exhibit becomes a permanent memorial to the lost tradition.
mcQuestions.explanation: The metaphor contrasts 'seed' (something that can grow and regenerate) with 'tomb' (a place where something dead is kept) — the exhibit prompts living revival.
mcQuestions.hint: A seed grows; a tomb stores the dead. Which does the essay prefer?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is described as 'the default logic of the museum'?
options: To educate, inspire, and empower communities.
To fundraise for endangered traditions.
To select, to freeze, and to display.
To archive, to distribute, and to teach.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states the museum's default logic is 'to select, to freeze, and to display' — all three of which work against living traditions.
mcQuestions.hint: Three infinitives in sequence describe the museum's logic — re-read paragraph four.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay describe whakapapa recitation?
options: A fixed archive of genealogical data performed identically each time.
A living negotiation of identity, shaped differently in each community and generation.
A formal ceremony managed by Māori government institutions.
An oral tradition that has already been successfully archived by museums.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay explicitly calls it 'a living negotiation of identity, shaped differently in each community and each generation' — the opposite of fixed data.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay contrasts 'fixed genealogical data' with something more dynamic.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay identify as the mechanism keeping a tradition alive?
options: Government funding for cultural festivals.
Museum exhibits that attract young visitors.
Generational friction — variations, disagreements, and adaptations passed imperfectly.
Digital recordings that preserve the tradition precisely.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues 'generational friction' — grandmothers insisting on variations, uncles changing names in stories — is what allows traditions to absorb new experience.
mcQuestions.hint: The final paragraph lists specific examples of what the essay calls 'the mechanism'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction is used to present the museum transfer as systemic rather than personal?
options: the tradition has been stripped of the only context
Grandmothers who insist on their own variation
young people have sought out remaining practitioners
A tradition survives not by being curated but by being used
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has been stripped' is present perfect passive — it omits the agent and treats the stripping as a consequence of a system, not a named individual.
mcQuestions.hint: The passive removes the agent; the essay uses this to critique a process, not a person.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'remains inhabitable by the living' mean in the essay's context?
options: The tradition has a physical building where people can live.
People can still safely practise the tradition in public.
The tradition stays flexible and meaningful enough for living people to use and identify with.
The tradition is stored in a place where people can visit it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Inhabitable' means capable of being lived in — used metaphorically to mean a tradition that real people can still meaningfully use, not just observe.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'inhabit' as something you do with a living space — how does that apply to a tradition?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to fossilise' mean in the context of traditions?
options: To turn a tradition into a scientific discovery.
To make a tradition rigid and no longer capable of living change.
To bury a tradition underground so it is forgotten.
To display a tradition in a natural history museum.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Fossilise' means to become rigid and dead like a fossil — when applied to traditions it means they stop changing and can no longer respond to living experience.
mcQuestions.hint: A fossil is something once alive, now preserved unchanged — what does that suggest about a tradition?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition signals a concession followed by a return to the main argument?
options: Furthermore, a museum exhibit can prompt a revival.
Granted, a museum exhibit can prompt a revival.
Therefore, a museum exhibit can prompt a revival.
In conclusion, a museum exhibit can prompt a revival.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted' signals a concession — the writer acknowledges a valid point from the opposing side before reasserting the main argument with 'Even so'.
mcQuestions.hint: Only one word here functions as a concession marker — look for the one that means 'I admit this is true'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains a consistently formal B2 register?
options: Museums are pretty cool and help keep old stuff alive, which is great.
A tradition survives not by being curated but by being used, argued over, adapted.
Like, grandmothers always have the best recipes and they should totally share them.
It's kinda true that museums can be helpful sometimes but also not really.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses parallel infinitive phrases in an analytical structure. No slang, hedging intensifiers, or colloquial expressions appear.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for precise vocabulary and parallel grammatical structure with no informal language.

options: Physical artefacts stored in museums.
Cultural buildings and monuments.
Practices, oral traditions, and knowledge passed between people rather than stored as objects.
Digital copies of ancient manuscripts.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Intangible' means unable to be physically touched or measured — intangible heritage refers to practices, knowledge, and performances rather than physical objects.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of what 'intangible' means — if you cannot touch it, it is intangible.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means 'the knowledge that lives in how a body performs a skill'?
options: collective memory
institutional archiving
embodied knowledge
generational continuity
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Embodied knowledge' refers to skills and understanding stored in physical practice — how to fold a leaf, how to hold a tool — rather than in words or documents.
mcQuestions.hint: The word 'embody' means to give a body to — embodied knowledge lives in the body, not the page.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why can no museum replicate the function of domestic practice?
options: Museums do not have enough staff to manage living traditions.
Museums only accept physical objects, not oral traditions.
The museum's purpose is to stabilise what practice keeps deliberately unstable.
Museums are too expensive for most communities to access.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay closes by stating that 'the museum's purpose is to stabilise what practice keeps deliberately unstable' — the two institutions are fundamentally opposed in purpose.
mcQuestions.hint: The final sentence of the essay states this directly.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best expresses a contrast between surface appearance and deeper reality?
options: who stays awake through the night watching the pot
the label reads correctly, the photographs are accurate — but the tradition has been stripped of the only context
these are genuinely valuable interventions
shaped differently in each community and each generation
mcQuestions.explanation: The two clauses use 'but' to contrast outward accuracy (label, photographs) with the real loss (stripped of context) — surface versus substance.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a 'but' that separates what appears correct from what is actually lost.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a tradition in your family or community that you think would be diminished if it were displayed in a museum? What would be lost in the transfer, and is there anything that might be gained?
